Choose MongoDB for: user profiles, catalogs, real-time analytics.

question: What is REST API authentication?
answer: Common REST API Authentication methods: API Keys (simple, include in header or query param), Basic Auth (base64 encoded user:pass — insecure without HTTPS), JWT (stateless tokens, signed, include in Authorization: Bearer header), OAuth 2.0 (delegated authorization, used for third-party logins), Session Cookies (stateful, server stores session). Best practice: always use HTTPS, short-lived tokens, refresh tokens for long sessions.

question: What is TypeScript?
answer: TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft. Adds: Type annotations, Interfaces, Generics, Enums, Decorators, Better IDE support. Compiles to plain JavaScript. Benefits: Catch type errors at compile time, Better refactoring, Self-documenting code, Great for large codebases. Used with: React, Angular (built with TS), Node.js, Next.js. File extension: .ts or .tsx.

question: What is Next.js?
answer: Next.js is a React framework for production-ready web applications. Features: Server-Side Rendering (SSR), Static Site Generation (SSG), Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR), API Routes (build backend in same project), File-based routing, Image Optimization, Built-in TypeScript support. Used by: Netflix, TikTok, GitHub, Twitch. Best choice for full-stack React applications.

question: What is Vue.js?
answer: Vue.js is a progressive JavaScript framework for building UIs. Features: Reactive data binding, Component-based architecture, Vue CLI, Vuex (state management), Vue Router, Composition API (Vue 3). Simpler learning curve than React. Single File Components (.vue) include template, script, style. Used by: Alibaba, Xiaomi, Adobe. Nuxt.js is Vue's Next.js equivalent for SSR.

question: What is Tailwind CSS?
answer: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework. Instead of pre-built components, it provides utility classes (flex, mt-4, text-center, bg-blue-500). Benefits: No naming CSS classes, Consistent design system, Highly customizable, Purges unused CSS (tiny bundle). Downsides: HTML can look cluttered with many classes. Compare: Bootstrap (component-based), Tailwind (utility-based). Works great with React, Next.js, Vue.

question: What is Redux?
answer: Redux is a state management library for JavaScript apps (commonly used with React). Core concepts: Store (single source of truth), Action (plain object describing what happened), Reducer (pure function that updates state), Dispatch (send action to reducer), Selector (read from store). Modern Redux uses Redux Toolkit (less boilerplate). Alternatives: Zustand, Jotai, Recoil, React Context + useReducer.

question: Tell me about your project.
answer: Project explanation structure: 1) Problem statement — what problem does it solve? 2) Tech stack — languages, frameworks, tools used. 3) Your role — what did you specifically build? 4) Key features — what makes it unique? 5) Challenges — what was difficult and how did you solve it? 6) Results/Impact — users, performance improvement, etc. Always have 2-3 solid projects ready. Push code to GitHub before interviews!

question: What is good coding practice?
answer: Good Coding Practices: Meaningful variable/function names, Single Responsibility Principle (each function does one thing), DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid), YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It), Write comments for WHY not WHAT, Unit tests, Proper error handling, Code reviews, Version control (Git), Consistent formatting (Prettier, Black for Python), SOLID principles.

question: What is SOLID principles?
answer: SOLID Principles for clean OOP design: S - Single Responsibility (one class, one job), O - Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), L - Liskov Substitution (subclass can replace parent without breaking code), I - Interface Segregation (no forced implementation of unused methods), D - Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions). Following SOLID makes code maintainable, testable, and scalable.

question: What is clean code?
answer: Clean Code is readable, maintainable code that other developers can understand. Principles: Meaningful names (getUserById not getU), Functions should be small and do one thing, No magic numbers (use constants), Avoid deep nesting, Consistent formatting, Delete dead code, Write self-documenting code, Minimal comments (good code explains itself). Book: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) — a must-read for every developer.

question: What is the internet?
answer: The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers communicating via standardized protocols (TCP/IP). Key components: Routers (forward packets), DNS (domain to IP), HTTP/HTTPS (web), SMTP (email), FTP (file transfer), Backbone networks (high-speed fiber). ISP (Internet Service Provider) connects users to the internet. WWW (World Wide Web) is a service built on top of the internet using HTTP.

question: What is IPv4 vs IPv6?
answer: IPv4: 32-bit address, ~4.3 billion addresses (example: 192.168.1.1). Running out due to internet growth — NAT (Network Address Translation) extends life. IPv6: 128-bit address, 340 undecillion addresses (example: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334). Features: No NAT needed, Auto-configuration, Better security (IPSec built-in). Transition: dual-stack (both IPv4+IPv6). Most modern systems support both.

question: What is subnetting?
answer: Subnetting divides a large network into smaller sub-networks. Subnet Mask: 255.255.255.0 (/24) means first 24 bits are network, last 8 bits are hosts → 256 addresses (254 usable). CIDR notation: 192.168.1.0/24. Benefits: Reduces broadcast traffic, Better security, Efficient IP usage. Practice: calculate network address, broadcast address, valid host range, number of subnets. Common in networking interviews.
